retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkeratosis of the Nipple and Areola -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The classification initially proposed by Levy-Franckel divides hyperkeratosis of the nipple and areola into 3 types: (1) epidermal nevus, with unilateral involvement of a single breast, usually in a Blaschko linear pattern (observed equally in both genders); (2) hyperkeratosis of the nipple and areola associated with ichthyosis, with bilateral involvement of the breasts (observed equally in both genders); and (3) a bilateral type, generally affecting both nipples and areolae and observed mainly in females in the second or third decade of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Morris Ritz et al. suggested a simple operative technique using two dermoglandular flaps. The inverted nipple is elevated using a skin hook. A perinipple "doughnut" of skin is de-epithelialized to a width of 3mm. Two longitudinal 1.5cm dermofibrous flaps are created attached to the nipple base. The nipple is elevated to its maximal length, and the ducts and fibrous tissue are teased out to release the nipple. A blunt dissector is used to create two tunnels in the deep tissue beneath the nipple, wherein the flaps are stitched at the nipple base fairly taut with 4-0 monocryl. The skin is closed with interrupted half-buried sutures. Sidewalls of grade 3 nipples are not closed and left to heal by epithelialization. A doughnut dressing with antibacterial ointment is used with the neonipple secured to the dressing with 4-0 silk for the first 5 days. This technique did not see any major postoperative events but allowed for a successful correction with a minimal, well-tolerated scar around the neonipple base. An advantage of this technique is the ease of revision correction in case of failure. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Yukun et al. treated all grades of nipple inversion for 10 years utilizing a nipple retractor made from the hollow end of a single-use syringe, then eight holes were punctured for sutures crossing the base, and the height of the retractor depended on the sizes of the nipple-areola complex and breast volume. Two sutures were made to cross beneath the base of the nipple to elevate the nipple, and the hollow retractor was placed on the areola with the nipple and four ends of the sutures in the center. Sutures were then passed through the prefabricated holes on the retractor base and were fixed with knots and suitable tension. The retractor was worn for 3-6 months and then could be removed. [8] Grade 1 and 2 inversions were treated more successfully than grade 3, but a significant advantage described was the prevention of lactiferous duct injury and preservation of breastfeeding.

retrieved_docs.title: Nipple-Areolar Complex Reconstruction -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: The personnel required to perform NAC reconstruction include the following: Plastic surgeon Surgical first assistant Surgical technician or operating room nurse Circulating or operating room nurse An anesthetist or anesthesiologist is required if general anesthesia is utilized during the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Inverted nipple correction is normally a very safe procedure. Patients most often experience swelling and sensitivity in the immediate postoperative period, which resolves uneventfully. Bleeding and wound infection are the most common surgical complications, though both are rare. Recurrence of nipple inversion is the most significant complication, and the greatest risk of recurrence is seen at 6 to 12 months postoperatively.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An inverted nipple is a clinical diagnosis. Investigations like mammography, ultrasound, or ductoscopic evaluation can be done when inverted nipples are associated with discharge/ ectasia/ malignancy. Findings may include treatable causes like underlying masses or infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: An inverted nipple is a condition defined as the nipple, which is retracted inwards, rather than pointing outwards, as is seen in normal anatomy. It can occur in both sexes and can be congenital or acquired. The projection of the inverted nipple lies beneath the areolar plane, as opposed to the normal anatomic position where it projects beyond the plane of the areolar breast. The appearance can be psychologically disturbing, as well as being problematic during breastfeeding in nursing women. As many as 10% to 20% of females are born with one or more inverted nipples, and they may be completely asymptomatic until breastfeeding. [1] The appearance can be cosmetically undesired and worrisome. The benign inverted nipple must be differentiated from primary breast malignancy. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of nipple inversion. Review nipple inversion as a medical condition and clinical presentation. Outline the management options available for nipple inversion. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the care of patients with nipple inversion and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Inverted nipples can be either unilateral or bilateral; congenital or acquired. Schwanger classified them as ”umbilicated” if intermittently inverted and “invaginated” if permanently inverted. [5] The surgical classification used today was described by Han and Hong. They classified them into 3 grades according to the degree of fibrosis, the ease of manipulation, and the extent of damage sustained by the lactiferous ducts. [5] [6] Grade 1 inverted nipples - These are called “shy nipples.” They have minimal or no fibrosis and demonstrate a soft-tissue sufficiency. The lactiferous ducts are normal, even with the retraction. These nipples are very amenable to manipulation and maintain projection for a considerable time, thus enabling breastfeeding easily, although initiation may pose some difficulty.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: An algorithm was developed by Olivaz-Maneyo and Berniz for the treatment of nipple inversions. [20] The algorithm takes into consideration the lactation wishes of the patient and the degree of severity of nipple inversion. Lactation wishes are particularly important to consider in detail, as the duct-damaging procedures may cause irreversible damage and result in a total inability to breastfeed in the affected breast.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Lee et al. proposed an internal suturing technique to close the soft-tissue defect, sometimes present after eversion. An inferior periareolar 5 to 7 o'clock incision was made, and a superior periareolar nipple flap was raised. A traction nylon suture was applied to the inverted nipple to guide the dissection of the fibrous bands and ducts. After release, the defect was obliterated by a superior and inferior 2- layer suturing of the internal sidewalls of the nipple together. This was a duct-damaging technique that the authors reported had high patient satisfaction and no residual inversion, but had implications on breastfeeding. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Nipple-Areolar Complex Reconstruction -- Nursing, Allied Health, and Interprofessional Team Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: The reconstructive procedures mentioned above are often performed on an outpatient basis. Patients should be seen in close follow-up, typically 7 to 10 days following surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Pathological/acquired nipple inversion after puberty or breast development is more worrisome for malignancy or other abnormalities. This is generally associated with nipple discharge (serous/bloody), nipple erosion, or a breast lump. It is important to obtain a detailed personal and family history in both male and female patients of any breast malignancy, as well as any confounding trauma history to the breast or chest, as scarring and fat necrosis can mimic malignancy. The latter is not likely to result in nipple discharge.

retrieved_docs.title: Nipple-Areolar Complex Reconstruction -- Technique or Treatment -- Internal or External Nipple Prosthetics
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperatively,  nipple-specific dressings and shields are placed to protect the reconstructed NAC and avoid compression, trauma, or infection, leading to loss of NAC projection. Various dressings and shields have been described in the literature, including nipple-specific plastic guards, donut-shaped sponges, occlusive dressings with antibiotics, silicone-based products, and modified ocular shields and protectors. [35] [36] [37] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkeratosis of the Nipple and Areola -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Mammography and breast ultrasound examination are usually without anomalies in hyperkeratosis of the nipple and areola.

retrieved_docs.title: Inverted Nipple -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Nipple inversion is a condition with cosmetic and psychological implications for the patient, and it is best managed with an interprofessional team approach. The treating clinician should be mindful of the primary reason for the correction, whether it is the establishment of functionality or improving the physical appearance. Proper patient counseling regarding the condition, along with the available treatment options, helps them make an informed choice about their management. The most important distinction is between benign, congenital, nipple inversion, and malignancy. There should be a low threshold for the surgeon to obtain a biopsy or other intervention to ensure malignancy is not overlooked.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2389
retrieved_docs.content: The breast part serves as the direct site for other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast, and within this category the procedure إزالة الحلمة الإضافية (removal of an accessory nipple) is identified as a primary mapped intervention. This indicates that interventions performed on the breast part, such as the removal of an accessory nipple, are classified under the broader group of therapeutic skin and breast procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: اللمفاوية الجذري) are specific modalities within the same therapeutic hierarchy. All mastectomy variants are members of both the malignant breast tumor SMQ and the set of therapeutic and diagnostic procedures for malignant diseases, and they are linked by the “completely_excised_anatomy_has_procedure” relationship to the anatomical structures they remove. Overall, the knowledge network connects diagnostic biopsies of the areola and nipple, the role of these breast parts as sites for cancer detection, and a hierarchical spectrum of surgical interventions—from targeted excisions of accessory or aberrant tissue to partial and total mastectomy with lymph‑node removal—under a unified framework of therapeutic procedures for malignant breast disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Excision of accessory breast is a surgical procedure, also referred to in Arabic as إزالة الحلمة الإضافية, that involves the removal of an accessory nipple or extra breast tissue. This intervention is performed to eliminate supernumerary breast tissue that may cause cosmetic concerns, discomfort, or potential complications for the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: Breast part is identified as prsní bradavky, which are the anatomical nipples of the breast.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1949
retrieved_docs.content: Areola and the nipple (prsní bradavky) are both anatomical components classified as breast parts. The areola is identified as a specific type of breast part, and likewise the nipple is recognized as a breast part. In the clinical context of breast cancer, the areola serves as a finding site for cancer of the breast, indicating that lesions or diagnostic signs may be observed on the areola. Additionally, breast cancer can have the nipple as a finding site, meaning that the disease may also present with observable findings at the nipple. These interconnections emphasize the importance of examining both the areola and the nipple during breast cancer assessment and underscore their roles as relevant anatomical locations for detecting breast malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 894
retrieved_docs.content: The areola is an anatomical region of the breast and is classified as a breast part, as is the nipple (prsní bradavky). Both the areola and the nipple can serve as finding sites for cancer of the breast, meaning that breast cancer may be detected in either of these locations. When a suspicious lesion is present in the areola, the procedure titled “Biopsy of lesion of areola” is performed directly on the areola as its procedural site. Recognizing that the areola and the nipple are distinct breast parts that may harbor cancerous findings helps clinicians target diagnostic biopsies appropriately and supports comprehensive breast examination and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 168
retrieved_docs.content: on skin and breast” include removal of accessory nipple (إزالة الحلمة الإضافية), excision of aberrant breast tissue, and excision of accessory breast, all of which are related to partial and total mastectomy. Overall, the knowledge network connects diagnostic biopsies of the areola and nipple, the anatomical sites of malignant breast disease, and a structured hierarchy of surgical interventions—from accessory‑nipple removal and limited breast‑mass excision to partial mastectomy, lumpectomy, quadrantectomy, sub‑cutaneous and bilateral mastectomy, and lymph‑node removal—within the broader context of therapeutic and diagnostic procedures for malignant breast conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1219
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola is performed at the direct procedure site of the areola, and Biopsy of lesion of nipple is performed at the direct procedure site of the nipple (prsní bradavky). Both the areola and the nipple (prsní bradavky) are distinct anatomical components classified as breast parts. Recognizing that the areola and the nipple are specific breast parts helps clinicians accurately identify the appropriate site for biopsy of lesions affecting these structures, ensuring precise diagnostic sampling and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of a lesion of the areola and biopsy of a lesion of the nipple are both classified as breast biopsies (خزعة الثدي) and are performed directly on the areola and nipple (prsní bradavky), which are common anatomic sites for breast cancer and therefore serve as key finding locations for malignant lesions. Diagnostic breast biopsies that reveal a malignant neoplasm confirm the presence of cancer of the breast (سرطان الثدي), while therapeutic and other skin‑or‑breast procedures—including excision of aberrant breast tissue and removal of accessory nipples (إزالة الحلمة الإضافية) and broader “other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast”—are performed to treat or manage breast pathology such as primary malignant neoplasm of the areola of female breast. In the oncologic taxonomy, primary malignant neoplasm of the areola of female breast is listed among parent entities of the genitourinary neoplasm classification system, alongside cancer of the cervix (سرطان عنق الرحم), cancer of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2558
retrieved_docs.content: The breast part functions as the direct site for a range of other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast, indicating that it is the anatomical location where such interventions are performed. Among these interventions, the excision of aberrant tissue of breast is specifically linked to the category of other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast, highlighting that removal of abnormal breast tissue is one of the therapeutic actions carried out at the breast part. This relationship underscores the role of the breast part as the focal point for both general skin‑breast therapeutic procedures and the specific surgical removal of atypical breast tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1947
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola and biopsy of lesion of nipple are both classified as خزعة الثدي, indicating that these procedures are specific forms of breast biopsy that target lesions of the areola and nipple respectively. Recognizing these as subtypes of خزعة الثدي helps clinicians select appropriate diagnostic approaches for lesions located in these distinct anatomical regions of the breast.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 138
retrieved_docs.content: tumor and to the vomer from other primary sites, each retaining the classifications of both “ورم خبيث” and “ورم خبيث أولي”. Female breast cancer management includes diagnostic interventions (breast biopsy, other diagnostic procedures) and surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy. The male reproductive system hierarchy places testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية) as a primary disorder, while penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي) represents malignancy of other male genital organs. Overall, the knowledge base interconnects organ‑specific primary and metastatic malignant neoplasms, their morphological classifications, anatomical finding sites (areola, nipple, seminal vesicles, vomer, nasal bone, thymus, etc.), and related clinical procedures, providing a unified framework for recognizing, classifying, and managing cancers across breast, genitourinary, head‑and‑neck, and other systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 686
retrieved_docs.content: Excision of aberrant tissue of breast is linked to the broader category of other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast, indicating that it is considered a type of surgical intervention aimed at abnormal breast tissue. The category other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast is directly mapped from the Arabic term إزالة الحلمة الإضافية, which denotes the removal of an accessory nipple. In addition, Excision of aberrant tissue of breast has a broader relationship with Excision of accessory breast, a specific procedure that is itself classified as إزالة الحلمة الإضافية. Together, these relationships illustrate that the removal of aberrant or accessory breast tissue, including accessory nipples, is encompassed within the general therapeutic approaches to skin and breast surgery, highlighting a cohesive framework for managing congenital or anomalous breast tissue abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1111
retrieved_docs.content: The biopsy of lesion of nipple is a direct medical procedure performed on the prsní bradavky (breast nipples).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: interventions for breast cancer such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of the breast are connected to the finding site “female breast” and to diagnostic procedures like breast biopsy. Excision of aberrant breast tissue is related to excision of accessory breast tissue, reinforcing the therapeutic link between anatomical sites and procedural actions. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that diverse organ‑specific cancers are unified under the concept of malignant tumors, that hierarchical classifications connect specific neoplasms (e.g., primary malignant neoplasm of the areola, testicular cancer, cervical cancer) to broader disease categories, and that diagnostic (biopsy) and therapeutic (excision, lumpectomy, accessory‑nipple removal) procedures are directly tied to the anatomical locations and pathological entities they address, thereby informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Female breast serves as the anatomical location where cancer of breast is found, with breast cancer defined as a malignant tumor of the nipple in females (ورم خبيث في الحلمة لدى الإناث). The female breast is therefore the primary anatomic site associated with this malignant nipple tumor. Surgical management of breast cancer commonly involves procedures performed on the female breast, specifically lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast. These relationships underscore that the female breast is both the site of disease manifestation and the target of operative interventions aimed at removing malignant tissue.
